summary: I was born in Ethiopia, specifically in Bale Robe, Oromia region. Ethiopia has an oppressive dictatorship that does not allow freedom of speech, and speaking freely could lead to terrible consequences. I left Ethiopia in 1997 right after high school because of political dangers, including kidnappings related to tribal conflicts. I miss the land, the weather, and my family there, but the political problems and lack of freedom make it hard to return.

Bale Robe is where I was born, located in the Oromia region of Ethiopia. It is part of my roots and where my family still lives. Although I left Bale Robe due to political oppression and safety concerns, I carry memories of that place with me. I hope one day to return to Bale Robe and reconnect with the land and people I grew up with.

I come from the Oromia region in Ethiopia, where Bale Robe is located. The region is part of my identity and upbringing. Unfortunately, the political climate in Oromia, like the rest of Ethiopia, is harsh and has forced me to leave for safety reasons. Despite the difficulties, I still feel connected to Oromia and long to visit again someday.

After leaving Ethiopia in 1997, I moved to Nairobi, Kenya, where I lived in refugee camps for over five years. Nairobi was where I met my wife, and we bonded over our shared refugee status. We fell in love and got married there in 2000. Nairobi was a pivotal place in my life, marking the transition from my homeland to a new chapter abroad.

Kenya became my home after I fled Ethiopia in 1997, and I stayed there for a little over five years. Compared to Ethiopia, Kenya was more stable despite its corruption, and the government managed things better. It was in Kenya that I met my wife and started building a new life. Kenya gave me refuge and hope during a difficult time before moving to the United States.

I arrived in the United States in 2003 after my wife moved there in 2000 and I applied for a marriage visa. I first lived in Minnesota, which was very different from Nairobi, especially with the cold winters that shocked me. The U.S. offered many opportunities, but I also faced challenges like racism and hard work in manufacturing before pursuing my dream in the medical field. I have built a family here and hope to provide a better future for my children despite the difficulties.

Minnesota is where I settled when I came to the United States in 2003 to join my wife. The cold winters were a big shock to me compared to Nairobi. My wife and I were happy to be reunited there, and the community helped set me up with an apartment, car, and furnished house. Minnesota became the place where I started working hard, first in manufacturing and later in healthcare, while raising our family.

Some of my old friends moved to Las Vegas, and they were more surprised by the culture shock than I was when I came to the United States. While I lived in Minnesota, I heard about their experiences in Las Vegas, which helped me appreciate the differences in how people adapt to new environments. Las Vegas represents another example of the challenges immigrants face adjusting to American life.
